description: This test case evaluates the system's ability to retrieve and synthesize information from multiple, separate document sections to answer a complex question.
question: Explain how the novel mask inspection system combines die-to-database (DB) and die-to-die (DD) detection flows to maximize usable sensitivity for detecting lithographically significant defects on multi-die masks, and discuss how the system addresses challenges such as focus and aberration compensation, flare correction in OPC features, and defect classification for lithographic significance.

gold_context_sources.section_title: 2. Description of the Related Art - DETAILED DESCRIPTION OF THE PREFERRED EMBODIMENTS
gold_context_sources.evidence: The embodiments described herein have a number of new and advantageous components aimed at resolving disadvantages of currently used die-to-database (DB) or die-to-die (DD) inspection methods and systems. One of these components is a DB detection flow in which the DB design and corresponding image from the first die of a multi-die mask are acquired... The computer subsystem is further configured for generating a die reference image for the first of the multiple dies by applying one or more parameters of the imaging subsystem learned by generating the DB reference image to the images generated by the imaging subsystem of one or more of the multiple dies other than the first of the multiple dies... One advantage of the embodiments described herein is that they combine aspects of DD and DB detection flows for multi-die layout masks with substantially high usable sensitivity using mask inspection tools... The embodiments described herein may therefore use DB modeling information to handle the tool related errors in traditional DD detection and may use the mDie reference image to handle the plate error and avoid modeling noise... In this manner, the embodiments described herein can maximize usable sensitivity by reducing rendering error, photomask imperfection and tool focus variation.

gold_context_sources.section_title: 2. Description of the Related Art - DETAILED DESCRIPTION OF THE PREFERRED EMBODIMENTS
gold_context_sources.evidence: In another embodiment, a characteristic of an optical proximity correction (OPC) feature in two or more of the multiple dies is designed to be different for flare correction in a lithography process performed with the mask, and generating the DB reference image includes generating different DB reference images for the two or more of the multiple dies on the mask using the design for the two or more of the multiple dies, respectively... The computer subsystem can alter the sensitivity of the inspection by filtering the defects that have been detected by DD detection based on identified flare correction locations... One advantage of the embodiments described herein is therefore that they provide capability for handling OPC variation caused by flare correction.

gold_context_sources.section_title: 2. Description of the Related Art - DETAILED DESCRIPTION OF THE PREFERRED EMBODIMENTS
gold_context_sources.evidence: In some embodiments, the computer subsystem is configured for acquiring information for one or more patterns closest to the first and second defects on the mask and classifying the first and second defects based on the information for the one or more patterns closest to the first and second defects on the mask, respectively... In a further embodiment, the computer subsystem is configured for determining classification categories for the first and second defects and assigning scores to the first and second defects based on the determined classification categories, and the scores indicate a severity of the first and second defects for a lithography process performed with the mask... Determining classification categories for the detected defects may also or alternatively include determining the printability of the detected defects on a wafer (or other specimen) if the wafer is printed with the mask in a lithography process... The computer subsystem may input one or more images generated for the detected defects into a model of the lithography process thereby generating simulated wafer images illustrating how the defects affect one or more patterns printed on the wafer in the lithography process.

ground_truth_answer:
The described mask inspection system innovatively integrates die-to-database (DB) and die-to-die (DD) detection flows to enhance the detection sensitivity of lithographically significant defects on multi-die masks. Initially, the system generates a DB reference image by simulating a physical mask from its design and modeling the image that an imaging subsystem would produce. This simulation incorporates nearfield modeling and partial coherent illumination modeling, calibrated to compensate for focus and aberration errors by adjusting imaging subsystem parameters until the simulated image closely matches actual acquired images from the first die. This DB detection flow effectively identifies systematic mask writer errors that repeat across multiple dies.

To complement this, the system generates a die reference image (mDie reference image) for the first die by applying the imaging parameters learned from the DB reference image to images from other dies on the mask, averaging out die-to-die variations and compensating for focus and aberration discrepancies using basis images derived from DB rendering. This combined approach overcomes limitations of traditional DD inspections by leveraging DB modeling for focus compensation, thereby maximizing usable sensitivity.

The system further addresses flare correction challenges where OPC features intentionally differ between dies to compensate for lithographic flare effects. It does so by generating different DB reference images for each die to reflect the designed variations, and by detecting flare correction locations through design comparisons. This enables the system to filter out or reduce sensitivity to differences caused by intentional OPC modifications, preventing nuisance defect detections and maintaining inspection accuracy.

Finally, the system classifies detected defects by acquiring detailed pattern information near the defect locations and assigning classification categories. It evaluates lithographic significance by simulating wafer printing processes using the defect images and their reference counterparts, predicting the printability and potential impact of defects. Scores indicating defect severity guide users to focus on critical defects, improving defect dispositioning, repair decisions, and ultimately ensuring higher mask quality and lithography yield.
